blurb: Two fat, fused leaves per body mimicking a split pebble, each with its own patterned window on top. A pair here in different markings — and once a year they split open to swap their whole skin for a fresh one.

care.light: Bright light with several hours of direct sun keeps them squat and patterned; too little and the bodies stretch tall and pale and lose their stone shape.
care.water: The whole game is timing. Water lightly in autumn and spring growth; keep them completely dry through summer dormancy and while the old leaves shrivel and the new pair emerges in winter. When in doubt, don't water.
care.soil: A very gritty, mostly-mineral mix in a deeper pot for the taproot.
care.temperature: Room temperature; keep above ~40°F and bone dry when cool.
care.dormancy: Reverse of most succulents — they grow in the cool months and rest in summer heat. Never water during the winter leaf-swap: the new body lives off the old one.
care.feeding: Essentially none; a very dilute low-nitrogen feed at most, once in autumn.
care.potting: They resent disturbance and go years between repots; a deep pot suits the taproot.
care.propagation: From seed (slow), or by dividing established clumps.
care.bloom: A daisy-like white or yellow flower squeezes out from the central slit in autumn.
care.troubles: Overwatering — especially out of season — is fatal; watch for a body that goes translucent and mushy.
